chunk_summary: Rehabilitation in SAM includes sensory stimulation and emotional support to improve neurodevelopment, caregiver engagement, and recovery markers like playfulness, alongside therapeutic feeding and addressing barriers to relapse.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of sensory stimulation and emotional support in SAM rehabilitation.
Learn how to create structured daily routines for caregivers and children during recovery.
Identify and address barriers to recovery, including maternal depression and food insecurity.
Recognize early markers of recovery and danger signs for relapse in SAM management.
key_facts: Sensory stimulation and emotional support should begin once the child is clinically stable.
Structured daily routines include 15–30 minutes of play sessions at least twice daily.
Improving playfulness is an early marker of recovery in SAM rehabilitation.
Barriers like maternal depression, food insecurity, and domestic violence should be screened and addressed.
Deterioration in activity is a danger sign, even if weight has not yet fallen.
reference_quote: Treat sensory stimulation and emotional support as a prescribed intervention that begins once the child is clinically stable. (Source: WHO Pocket book of hospital care for children)
document_key_topics: Barriers to recovery and relapse prevention
Markers of recovery in SAM rehabilitation
